summary: Adversarial verification of docs/PROTOCOL.md (crypto + web-platform claims)

fatal_flaws: Media nonce construction (§5.2) has zero safety margin and rests on an invariant the spec never states or enforces. nonce = u32(chunkIndex) || 8 zero bytes is deterministic, and the per-transfer key k_t is derived *solely* from transferId (HKDF/HMAC over transferId). So (k_t, nonce) is a pure function of (transferId, chunkIndex). If the same transferId is ever used a second time with different chunk plaintext, you get classic GCM nonce reuse: keystream cancellation (I reproduced C1^C2 == P1^P2 in WebCrypto) AND GCM auth-key recovery enabling chunk forgery. Two concrete, unguarded reuse paths: (a) RETRY/RE-OFFER -- §5.2 says a failed transfer 'aborts (no resume)'; a UI retry that reuses the same transferId (natural if the id is minted at file-selection) re-encrypts chunk 0..n under the same key+nonces, and image re-encode (canvas->WebP) / voice-video re-record produces *different* plaintext -> catastrophic. (b) CROSS-SENDER COLLISION -- transferId generation is unspecified; if it is a per-peer counter/timestamp rather than a CSPRNG value, two senders in the room can pick the same transferId, derive the same k_t, and both start at nonce 0 on different plaintext. The deterministic-counter-nonce design is only safe if transferId is guaranteed globally unique per attempt, and the spec neither requires nor warns of this. Must-fix before implementation: mandate transferId = fresh 128-bit crypto.getRandomValues per *attempt* (never reused on retry/re-offer), AND/OR add a random 8-byte per-transfer nonce salt carried in the offer (nonce = u32(chunkIndex) || salt8) so AES-GCM nonce uniqueness does not depend on transferId discipline at all -- the latter is the robust belt-and-suspenders fix.
amendments: channelFingerprint (§3.4 hello proof) is undefined -- it appears exactly once in the whole spec (grep-confirmed) and is the load-bearing anti-replay binding. The hello is already encrypted+signed under k_msg, so a key-less signaling operator cannot *forge* a hello; the only attack is REPLAYING a captured genuine hello onto a different DTLS channel to appear as a key-confirmed member. That is defeated ONLY if channelFingerprint binds to the actual connection. Define it concretely as e.g. sorted(localDtlsCertFingerprint, remoteDtlsCertFingerprint) read from the negotiated SDP, with the *receiver recomputing and verifying* the proof against its own view of that pair. If an implementer instead plugs in roomId/transferId/a constant, the 'knocking without the key' lockout collapses to a replay. Without this definition the §3.4 security property is unspecified.

seq replay-vs-liveness across reconnects is unspecified (§2). The spec says senders use a 'per-sender monotonic counter' and receivers keep 'high-water marks', but never states whether either side PERSISTS. Two failure modes: (1) if the receiver high-water resets on reload, replay protection is lost across reloads (operator/member replays old signed envelopes, accepted as new); (2) if the sender resets seq to 0 on reload/reconnect but the receiver keeps its high-water (e.g. 500), every post-reload message (seq 1,2,3 < 500) is silently dropped -- the peer is censored after refresh. Specify: persist receiver high-water per (roomId, senderDeviceId) in IndexedDB; and use a signed monotonic (epoch, counter) where epoch is a persisted/monotonic boot id so a fresh session legitimately resets counter to 0 while a replayed old epoch is rejected. chat messageId dedupe partially masks (1) for chat, but edit/delete/reaction/ack/hello rely on seq.

Internal inconsistency in media key derivation: §1.2 (lines 33-36) defines per-transfer keys as importKey(HMAC(k_mediaBase, 'media|'+transferId)) as AES-256-GCM, but §5.2 line 177 still says k_t = HKDF(fragment key, info='mindline/v2/media/'+transferId). Different KDF, different input key (k_mediaBase vs raw fragment key), different label string. The spec's own rule 'if code and this document disagree, one is a bug' applies internally. Reconcile to one; the HMAC-base form is preferable (the §1.2 note that HKDF *material* CryptoKeys do not reliably survive IndexedDB structured clone is plausible and the reason for the switch -- update §5.2 to match).

Canonicalization is inconsistent. The AAD was hardened to length-prefixed encoding (u32be(len)||field) to kill delimiter games, but the hello proof HMAC(k_auth,'hello|'+deviceId+'|'+channelFingerprint) (§3.4), the signaling HMAC over deviceId+'|'+clientId+'|'+roomId (§3.1), the media AAD transferId+'|'+chunkIndex (§5.2), and storage AAD roomId|storage|page (§4) all still use '|'-delimited string concatenation. roomId is user/path-influenced (server accepts 4-100 chars, no charset restriction) and could contain '|'. Impact is low because k_auth/k_storage are per-room (cross-room confusion needs the same key) and clientId is server-assigned, but for uniformity and defense-in-depth apply the same length-prefixed encoding to every keyed-MAC/AAD input.

Nonce-bound framing understates the shared-key reality (§2). k_msg is derived once from the room key and shared by ALL participants; everyone encrypts under the SAME k_msg. So the GCM random-96-bit-IV collision bound is over the room-wide total of msg encryptions, not 'per-sender'. It is still far below the NIST ~2^32-invocation guidance at any realistic chat volume (q^2/2^97 is negligible), and per-keystroke eph traffic uses a separate k_eph, so the conclusion holds -- but reword to reflect that the bound is room-wide and note that AAD does NOT prevent keystream reuse on an IV collision (GCM keystream depends only on key+nonce).

Storage/eviction residual is missing from the honesty pass. The spec promises 'revisiting /{roomId} without the fragment still opens local history' (§1.2) and stable deviceId TOFU (§1.3), both staked on IndexedDB persistence of CryptoKeys. Safari ITP evicts all script-writable storage (IndexedDB included) after 7 days of no interaction for non-installed sites -- wiping persisted subkeys, encrypted history, AND the ECDSA device keypair, so the fragment-less reopen breaks and deviceId/TOFU identity churns. §4 requests navigator.storage.persist() only 'on first media receipt', so text-only rooms never request persistence and are most eviction-prone. Fix: request persist() at room join / key creation, not just on media; and add the 7-day Safari eviction (history loss + identity churn) to §6 / PRIVACY per the D7 honesty rule. Also validate the §1.2 structured-clone-of-CryptoKey claim across the Chrome/Firefox/Safari matrix in the test contract (currently §7 only tests HKDF vectors, not key persistence/round-trip through IndexedDB).

Blob-at-rest AAD is unspecified (§4 line 151): media blobs are 'encrypted with k_storage, keyed {roomId}/{transferId}' but no AAD is given, while message pages get AAD roomId|storage|page. Bind blob AAD to roomId|transferId (length-prefixed) so a stored blob ciphertext cannot be relocated to a different transferId/room slot.

Minor/eph: t='eph' envelopes (drafts, presence) are encrypted under k_eph but unsigned (§2), so any room member can spoof another member's live draft or presence ping. The spec calls drafts 'transient UI', which is a defensible tradeoff, but note presence-spoofing (faking a peer online/offline) explicitly as an accepted residual since presence drives participant UI.

§5.3 WebP mandate fails on 2 of 3 engines: canvas.toBlob('image/webp') is unsupported in Safari AND Firefox (silently falls back to PNG, blowing the q0.82 compression and the ≤12 KB webp thumbnail budget). Specify feature detection (check resulting blob.type) with image/jpeg fallback for both the re-encode and the thumbnail, and make the offer's thumb mime explicit rather than hardcoding webp.

§5.2 framing arithmetic is wrong: overhead is 32 B (4 B chunkIndex + 12 B nonce + 16 B GCM tag), not 28 B. 15360+32=15392 is still under 16384, so the design is safe — just fix the number. Also note 16 KiB is conservative, not the actual modern floor: current engines negotiate 256 KiB via SDP max-message-size (64 KiB assumed when absent); keeping 15 KB chunks is fine.

§3.5 sync pages contradict the doc's own size model: a 48 KB pre-encryption page becomes a ~65 KB JSON envelope (base64 ciphertext inflates 4/3) on the chat DataChannel — above the 64 KiB SDP-default maxMessageSize and 4x the doc's stated 16 KB floor, and far above the relay server's 16 KB maxPayload if sync ever relays. Cap pages so the final wire envelope stays under your chosen floor (~11 KB plaintext if relayable, ~40 KB if DataChannel-only and you accept the 64 KiB default), and state explicitly that sync never runs over relay.

§3.1 HMAC field placement must be specified: signaling-server.js rebuilds every relayed message as {type, data: data.data, fromId} (offer/answer/ice-candidate lines 327-344, relay lines 347-384) and DROPS all other top-level fields; 'join' relays nothing client-supplied (peer-joined carries only clientId). The deviceId+hmac survive relay only if nested inside the data payload — say so, and define which message types carry them (the 'announce' after reconnect must be an offer/answer or relay broadcast, since join cannot carry it to peers).

§3.4/§3.6 internal contradiction: key confirmation (hello+proof) is defined only 'on chat channel open', but relay-of-last-resort has no channel — per §3.4, messages from unverified peers are dropped, so relayed messages from a peer that never completed a DataChannel hello would never be accepted. Define hello-over-relay (note channelFingerprint has no meaning there; bind to clientId or DTLS cert from SDP instead) or the relay path is dead code.

§1.2/§5.2 contradiction: the raw fragment key is 'imported once as HKDF material, then immediately discarded' and 'never persisted', yet §5.2 derives per-transfer k_t from the fragment key at transfer time. Persist the imported non-extractable HKDF base CryptoKey in IndexedDB alongside the four subkeys (HKDF CryptoKeys are structured-cloneable in all current browsers) — otherwise media transfer is impossible in fragment-less revisit sessions and after the stated discard.

§5.2 backpressure: bufferedamountlow fires only on a downward crossing of the threshold, so the pause(>1 MB)/wait pattern deadlocks if the buffer drains below 256 KB between the bufferedAmount check and listener attachment. Require attaching a persistent listener before sending (or re-checking bufferedAmount after attach). Also document that libwebrtc force-closes/throws when bufferedAmount exceeds its ~16 MB internal cap, so the 1 MB pause is also a correctness requirement, not just tuning.

§5.3 cross-engine playback is overstated: '<audio> handles playback' is unsafe for Chrome/Firefox-recorded webm/opus received on Safari — WebM-Opus playback was inconsistent through Safari 18.3 (Opus-in-Ogg only added in 18.4; Opus-in-MP4 still rejected). Safari-recorded audio/mp4(AAC)/video/mp4(H.264) plays everywhere, but the reverse direction needs an explicit minimum-Safari-version statement plus an E2E cross-engine playback test (record on Chromium, assert playback on WebKit) and a documented fallback (e.g., WebAudio decode or sender-side AAC option).

§5.3 EXIF handling: pin imageOrientation:'from-image' explicitly (it is the spec default and applied by current engines — Safari fully 18.5+, partial from 15.5; Firefox full keyword support only ~144+; pass nothing or feature-detect the keyword to avoid throws on older Firefox), and extend the E2E test to assert correct rotation of an orientation-tagged JPEG, not just EXIF absence. Also drop or narrow 'skip re-encode for already-small non-JPEG files': PNG (eXIf chunk) and WebP (EXIF/XMP chunks) can carry GPS, so skipping re-encode for them leaks the metadata the section claims to strip 'by construction'.

§4 persistence timing: request navigator.storage.persist() when room keys are first persisted, not on first media receipt — Safari's ITP evicts all script-writable storage (including IndexedDB keys) after 7 days without user interaction unless the origin is persisted (Safari 17+ supports persist/estimate; persisted origins are exempt). As written, the §1.2 'revisit without fragment opens history' claim silently fails on Safari for rooms that never received media.

§1.3 rationale is stale: Ed25519 is now Baseline newly available (Safari 17, Firefox 129, Chrome 137 / May 2025), so 'universal WebCrypto support' is no longer the differentiator it was; P-256 remains the right call for installed-base reach (older Safari/Chrome), but reword the parenthetical to cite installed base rather than absent support.

§3.3 channels: state explicitly that media-{transferId} channels are created in-band (DCEP — no SDP renegotiation needed) and must be closed by both sides after complete/abort. Stream IDs (65,534 theoretical; even/odd split by DTLS role) are recycled only after the close handshake completes, so leak-free close discipline is what prevents id exhaustion in long-lived rooms — a worthwhile one-line requirement.
Checklist note: visualViewport is not mentioned anywhere in PROTOCOL.md, so there was no claim to verify; if the UI layer depends on it, that contract lives outside this document.
